nativeRange.habitat: Streams and slow-moving freshwater bodies on the island of Sumatra, where it grows along shaded stream margins and can experience seasonal emersed/submerged transitions.

avoidWith: Silver Dollars and other plant-eating fish

summary: Cryptocoryne Pontederiifolia is a rosette plant native to Sumatra, known for its long, olive-green leaves and forgiving care requirements. It grows well in low to moderate light and is well suited to the midground or background of a freshwater planted aquarium.
careNotes:
Cryptocoryne pontederiifolia is a rosette plant from the island of Sumatra, belonging to the family Araceae (the same family as peace lilies and taro). It typically reaches 10–25 cm in height in the aquarium, producing long leaves that taper to a point or broaden into an arrowhead or heart shape; the foliage is usually olive-green, occasionally with pink-tinged undersides under stronger light. Like all Cryptocoryne species, it is a heavy root feeder, drawing most of its nutrition from the substrate rather than the water column. Planting in a nutrient-rich aquarium soil and adding root tabs near the root zone will give it the best start. When planting, bury the roots but NOT the crown — the point where the leaves emerge from the thick horizontal stem (the rhizome). Burying the crown leads to rot.

CRYPT MELT WARNING: like other Cryptocoryne species, C. pontederiifolia is prone to a phenomenon known as crypt melt — a sudden collapse of existing leaves that can happen when the plant is newly introduced, after a large water change, or following any significant shift in water parameters. The plant may shed most or all of its leaves over just a few days, which can look alarming. This is a normal acclimation response and not a sign the plant is dying. Do NOT pull up the rhizome and roots; given stable conditions, the plant typically regrows new leaves adapted to its new environment within 2–4 weeks. To reduce the chance of melt, maintain consistent water parameters and avoid moving an established plant once it has settled in.
breedingNotes: Cryptocoryne pontederiifolia spreads primarily through runners (stolons) — thin stems that creep along or just below the substrate and produce daughter plants alongside the parent. Once a daughter plant has developed several leaves and a healthy root system, it can be gently separated from the runner and replanted elsewhere. Rhizome division is also possible: a healthy rhizome can be cut into sections, with each section retaining leaves and roots before being replanted. Like other Cryptocoryne species, C. pontederiifolia can flower when grown above the waterline (emersed), producing the hooded flower structure (a spathe enclosing a central spike) characteristic of the Araceae family. Seed propagation is not practical in the home aquarium.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed Sumatran origin, lanceolate olive-green leaves, moderate lighting requirement, midground-to-background placement, nutrient-rich-substrate preference, melt sensitivity under unstable parameters, and temperature range 68-82 °F (20-28 °C).

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Araceae), pH 6-7.5, slow growth rate, low-to-medium lighting, midground-to-background placement, easy care level, quality-aquarium-soil recommendation, optional CO2, and propagation by rhizome cutting/splitting. Lists 'Borneo' as native range, which conflicts with Modern Aquarium and the established taxonomic record for this Sumatran species; we have used Sumatra.

additional.name: Aquarium Lesson
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Araceae), temperature 22-25 °C, pH 6-7.5, kH 2-14 dKH, low light demand, slow growth, rosette form, midground-to-background placement, easy care level, and propagation by daughter plants/runners.
